programs.name: Big Sky Waiver Program
programs.tagline: Home care services for Montana seniors who qualify for nursing home level care but want to stay home

localEntities.name: Mountain Pacific Quality Health Foundation
County OPA offices

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home, Big Sky Waiver can provide personal assistance, adult day health, respite care, and other services to make that possible. The program covers everything from help with bathing and dressing to environmental modifications and specialized medical equipment — services that would otherwise cost thousands monthly out of pocket.

Your parent must qualify for Medicaid and meet Montana's nursing facility level of care assessment. Income limit: $967/month for individuals in 2025. Asset limit: $2,000 (primary home doesn't count). Currently has a waiting list, so applying early matters even if your parent doesn't need services immediately.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must qualify for nursing facility level of care through a clinical assessment — meaning your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or has cognitive impairment requiring supervision
otherRequirements: Montana resident
Financially eligible for Medicaid
Must have unmet care needs that only Big Sky Waiver services can resolve
Need at least one waiver service monthly

applicationGuide.summary: Call Mountain Pacific at (800) 219-7035 for a referral and apply for Medicaid through your county office — processing time varies and there's currently a waiting list

steps.title: Call for waiver referral
steps.description: Contact Mountain Pacific Quality Health Foundation at (800) 219-7035 or (406) 443-4020 to start the Big Sky Waiver referral process

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid
steps.description: Visit or call your county Office of Public Assistance (OPA) to apply for Medicaid financial eligibility — this is required before waiver approval

steps.title: Complete nursing facility assessment
steps.description: Schedule the nursing facility level of care assessment — this can be done in-person or over the phone to determine if your parent's care needs qualify

steps.title: Wait for approval and placement
steps.description: If approved, you'll be placed on the waiting list — priority is given based on urgency of need and risk factors

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — varies by case complexity and current waitlist length
applicationGuide.waitlist: Currently has a waiting list — placement depends on priority level and regional availability
applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if your parent doesn't need services immediately — the waiting list means earlier applications get priority when services become available

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for applicant and spouse
Medicare card (both Part A and Part B)
Proof of Montana residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or state-issued document)
Birth certificate or other proof of age
Most recent Social Security award letter
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Property deeds and tax statements
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with cash values
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Medical records documenting functional limitations
Current medication list and prescribing physician information
Documentation of current care arrangements and unmet needs

contacts.label: Mountain Pacific (Big Sky Waiver referrals)
contacts.description: Primary contact for Big Sky Waiver referrals and questions

contacts.label: Mountain Pacific local line
contacts.description: Local Montana number for Big Sky Waiver inquiries

contacts.label: Montana 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all Montana social services and benefits

applicationNotes: For married couples, if only one spouse applies for the waiver, both spouses' assets are still counted as jointly owned — Medicaid planning may be needed
The primary home is exempt from asset limits regardless of value, but other property may need to be addressed
Priority for services is given to people at immediate risk of nursing home placement or safety concerns
You can apply for Medicaid and request the waiver referral simultaneously — don't wait for Medicaid approval to start the waiver process

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than $2,000?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is completely exempt from Big Sky Waiver asset limits regardless of value. Your parent can own a $500,000 home and still qualify. However, other assets like second homes, rental properties, or excess vehicles would count toward the $2,000 limit.

faqs.question: How long is the waiting list really?
faqs.answer: The state doesn't publish specific wait times, and it varies by region and your parent's priority level. People at immediate risk of nursing home placement or with safety concerns get priority. Call Mountain Pacific at (800) 219-7035 for current wait time estimates in your area.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can help with the application process. You'll need to contact Mountain Pacific for the waiver referral and help your parent apply for Medicaid at the county OPA office. If your parent cannot make decisions, you may need legal guardianship or power of attorney documentation.

faqs.question: What if my parent has too much income for Medicaid but needs these services?
faqs.answer: Montana has Medicaid spend-down rules that may help. If medical expenses reduce your parent's countable income below $967/month, they could still qualify. Contact your county OPA office to discuss spend-down calculations — sometimes even nursing home costs can count toward spend-down.

faqs.question: Can my parent get Big Sky Waiver services while also receiving other benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes, Big Sky Waiver works alongside other benefits like Social Security, Medicare, SNAP, and housing assistance. However, you cannot receive Big Sky Waiver services and be in a nursing home at the same time — it's specifically for community-based care.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's condition gets worse after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Your parent's care plan can be modified as needs change. The case manager will reassess service needs regularly and can add services or increase hours. If your parent eventually needs full nursing home care, they would transition off the waiver but remain Medicaid-eligible for facility coverage.

faqs.question: My spouse doesn't need waiver services — how do income and asset limits work?
faqs.answer: When only one spouse applies for Big Sky Waiver, the individual income limit ($967/month) applies and the other spouse's income is disregarded. However, both spouses' assets are still counted together toward the $2,000 limit, which often requires careful Medicaid planning.

faqs.question: What specific services will my parent actually receive?
faqs.answer: Services depend on your parent's assessed needs and could include personal assistance with bathing and dressing, homemaker services, adult day health, respite care for family caregivers, environmental modifications like ramps or grab bars, specialized medical equipment, or transportation to medical appointments. The case manager develops an individualized care plan.

programs.name: Community First Choice Services (CFCS) / Personal Care Services (PCS)
programs.tagline: Get paid help for your parent's daily care at home — bathing, dressing, and medication management covered by Medicaid.

localEntities.name: Provider Type 12 enrollment via Optum/Conduent; examples: Summit Independent Living for self-directed

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or taking medications but wants to stay home instead of moving to a nursing home, Montana's CFCS and PCS programs provide paid caregivers through Medicaid. Your parent gets a personal care attendant who comes to their home — and you can even become their paid caregiver if you're an adult child.

The program has two tiers: Personal Care Services covers basic assistance with daily activities, while Community First Choice Services adds extras like emergency response systems and community outings for parents who need nursing-home-level care. Because everyone approved for PCS gets automatically reviewed for CFCS, 95% of enrollees end up in the higher tier with more comprehensive services.

Income limit: $967/month for a single person (2025). Asset limit: $2,000 individual, $3,000 couple. Your parent must be Medicaid-eligible and demonstrate they need help with activities of daily living through a nurse assessment.

exemptAssets: Primary home (up to $730,000 equity if intent to return)
Home where spouse or disabled child lives

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Health condition limits ability to perform activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, medication management). Must pass nurse assessment by Mountain Pacific Quality Health (MPQH).
otherRequirements: Montana resident
Medicaid eligible
Able to direct care or have personal representative
Health care professional authorization

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Medicaid office for screening, then complete a nurse assessment — processing time not specified in state resources.

steps.title: Contact Medicaid office
steps.description: Visit medicaid.mt.gov to find your local office or call for initial screening and Medicaid eligibility confirmation.

steps.title: Complete nurse assessment
steps.description: Mountain Pacific Quality Health (MPQH) will schedule a nurse to assess your parent's functional needs and determine PCS or CFCS eligibility.

steps.title: Get health care authorization
steps.description: Your parent's doctor must provide written authorization confirming they need personal care assistance.

steps.title: Choose service model
steps.description: Select self-directed care (you manage and can hire family caregivers through agencies like Summit Independent Living) or agency-based care.

steps.title: Complete Provider Type 12 enrollment
steps.description: Enroll through Optum/Conduent system with your chosen provider agency to begin receiving services.

applicationGuide.tip: Everyone approved for PCS gets automatically reviewed for CFCS with additional services — don't apply separately for both tiers.

documentsNeeded: Social Security card
Medicare card (both parts)
Proof of Montana residency (utility bill or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents if applicable
Property deeds and tax statements
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with cash values
Health care professional authorization for personal care
Medical documentation showing functional limitations
Legal documents if using a personal representative
Proof of any other income sources
Documentation of intent to return home if temporarily absent

contacts.label: Montana Medicaid Information
contacts.description: Visit medicaid.mt.gov for local office contact information

contacts.label: Montana 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all social services and program information

applicationNotes: Spouses, parents, and legal guardians cannot be paid caregivers — only other family members like adult children or ex-spouses
Self-directed option allows you to hire and manage your parent's caregivers, including family members, through participating agencies
Provider agencies cover specific counties and reservations — onsite visits required for enrollment in your area
Home equity over $730,000 may affect eligibility unless spouse or disabled child lives in the home

contentSections.title: PCS vs. CFCS: What's the Difference?
contentSections.content: Personal Care Services (PCS) covers basic assistance with bathing, dressing, toileting, transfers, and medication reminders. Community First Choice Services (CFCS) includes everything in PCS plus Personal Emergency Response System, community integration activities, yard hazard removal, and correspondence assistance. CFCS requires Nursing Facility Level of Care determination (meaning your parent needs the same level of daily help provided in nursing homes), while PCS just needs medically necessary assistance. Since 95% of PCS enrollees qualify for CFCS after automatic review, most families end up with the more comprehensive tier.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limits?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent lives in the home or intends to return, home equity up to $730,000 is exempt. The home is also fully exempt if a spouse, minor child, or disabled adult child of any age lives there. However, Montana may pursue estate recovery on the home after your parent's death to recoup Medicaid costs.

faqs.question: Can I become my parent's paid caregiver through this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you're an adult child, you can be hired as a paid caregiver through the self-directed option. However, spouses, parents, and legal guardians cannot be paid caregivers. You'll need to work with agencies like Summit Independent Living to manage the employment paperwork and payments.

faqs.question: What's the difference between self-directed and agency-based care?
faqs.answer: Self-directed means you choose, hire, train, and supervise your parent's caregivers (including family members) with support from agencies like Summit Independent Living. Agency-based means the agency provides pre-screened caregivers. Self-directed offers more control and flexibility, while agency-based requires less management from your family.

faqs.question: How many hours of care will my parent receive?
faqs.answer: Hours depend on your parent's assessed needs during the MPQH nurse evaluation. The assessment looks at how much help your parent needs with each activity of daily living and creates an individualized care plan. More complex needs typically result in more authorized hours.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply for both PCS and CFCS at the same time?
faqs.answer: No need to apply separately. Everyone approved for PCS automatically gets reviewed for CFCS eligibility. If your parent qualifies for nursing-home-level care, they'll be upgraded to CFCS with additional services. The assessment process covers both programs simultaneously.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's condition gets worse after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Your parent can request a reassessment through their provider agency or MPQH if their care needs increase. The new assessment may authorize additional hours or services. Similarly, if needs decrease, the plan will be adjusted accordingly during regular reviews.

faqs.question: Does this program affect my parent's other Medicaid benefits?
faqs.answer: No, CFCS and PCS are additional Medicaid services that work alongside regular Medicaid health coverage. Your parent keeps all their existing Medicaid benefits including doctor visits, prescriptions, and hospital care. These programs specifically add the personal care component.

faqs.question: What if there are no provider agencies in my parent's area?
faqs.answer: Provider agencies must cover specific counties and reservations with onsite visits required. If coverage is limited in your area, contact the Montana Medicaid office to discuss available options or agencies willing to serve your parent's location. Rural areas may have fewer choices but should still have at least one participating provider.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: Complete healthcare and support services under one program for parents who need nursing home level care but want to stay home
programs.intro:
If your parent needs extensive daily help but wants to avoid a nursing home, PACE provides all medical care, medications, therapy, and support services through one coordinated team. Because PACE covers everything under one program, your parent won't need Medicare cards, copays, or coordination between separate doctors — one interdisciplinary team manages all care.

PACE has no income or asset limits, but your parent must be certified as needing nursing facility level of care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar needs) and must live in a PACE service area. Once enrolled, participants never pay deductibles or copays for any PACE-provided care, medication, or service.

Availability is limited in Montana — PACE operates only in select counties, not statewide. The average participant is 76 years old with multiple chronic conditions, and about 90% qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid.

summary: Must live in PACE service area
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by Montana as needing nursing facility level of care — the kind of daily supervision and assistance typically provided in a nursing home, such as help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility
otherRequirements: Must live in the service area of a PACE organization
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage (Part C), Medicare prepayment plan, or Medicare prescription drug plan
Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Cannot be enrolled in hospice services

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE program directly — no standardized application form exists.

steps.title: Find Montana PACE programs
steps.description: Use Medicare's Find a PACE Plan tool at medicare.gov or visit npaonline.org to locate PACE organizations operating in your parent's county.

steps.title: Contact the local PACE program
steps.description: Call the PACE organization serving your area directly. Each program manages its own enrollment process.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: A nurse will conduct an in-home assessment to determine if your parent meets Montana's nursing facility level of care requirement.

steps.title: Receive state certification
steps.description: Montana must formally certify that your parent qualifies for nursing home level care before PACE enrollment can proceed.

steps.title: Disenroll from conflicting plans
steps.description: If enrolled in Medicare Advantage (Part C) or prescription drug plans, your parent must disenroll before joining PACE.

applicationGuide.tip: Geographic availability is the biggest barrier — verify PACE operates in your parent's specific area before starting the assessment process.

contacts.label: Medicare General Information
contacts.description: General Medicare questions and PACE program information

contacts.label: Montana Department of Health
contacts.description: State certification and nursing facility level of care questions

applicationNotes: PACE is not available statewide in Montana — you must verify a program operates in your parent's specific county before pursuing eligibility
Medicaid beneficiaries receive full coverage while non-Medicaid participants pay a monthly premium for long-term care services
Your parent cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage and PACE simultaneously — disenrollment from Medicare Advantage is required
State certification of nursing facility level of care is mandatory and separate from meeting the functional criteria
relatedPrograms: Montana Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS)
Medicare Part A and Part B

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their Medicare supplements and prescription plans when they join PACE?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent must disenroll from Medicare Advantage (Part C) plans, Medicare prescription drug plans, and Medicare prepayment plans before joining PACE. PACE becomes their comprehensive coverage for all medical services and medications.

faqs.question: What if my parent has too much income or assets for Medicaid but still needs PACE services?
faqs.answer: PACE has no income or asset limits for enrollment. However, if your parent doesn't qualify for Medicaid, they'll pay a monthly premium for the long-term care portion of PACE services. Medicare will still cover the medical services portion.

faqs.question: How do I know if my parent really needs 'nursing facility level of care'?
faqs.answer: Montana defines this as needing the kind of daily supervision and care typically provided in a nursing home — help with bathing, dressing, medication management, mobility, or similar activities. A PACE nurse will conduct an in-home functional assessment to make this determination.

faqs.question: Can I apply for PACE while my parent is still in the hospital?
faqs.answer: Yes, and hospital discharge planners often help initiate the PACE application process. However, your parent must be able to live safely in the community with PACE services at the time of enrollment — they cannot be enrolled while requiring ongoing hospitalization.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a county that doesn't have PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE enrollment ends if you move outside the service area. Your parent would need to re-enroll in traditional Medicare, find new healthcare providers, and potentially apply for other long-term care programs in the new location.

faqs.question: Can my parent choose their own doctors and specialists under PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE provides all care through its interdisciplinary team of employed or contracted providers. Your parent cannot see outside specialists or use outside pharmacies unless the PACE team determines the service isn't available within their network.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get approved for PACE in Montana?
faqs.answer: Processing times vary by local PACE organization and depend on completing the functional assessment and receiving state certification. Contact the specific PACE program in your area for current enrollment timelines.

faqs.question: What if my parent's care needs change after enrolling in PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE conducts regular reassessments and adjusts services as needs change. If your parent no longer meets nursing facility level of care requirements, they may be disenrolled from PACE and returned to traditional Medicare.

programs.name: Medicaid for the Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 65+, blind, or disabled, they may qualify for comprehensive health coverage including long-term care services

localEntities.name: Local DPHHS Human and Community Services Division offices statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65 or older, blind, or disabled, Montana's ABD Medicaid can provide comprehensive health coverage when income is limited. This covers everything from doctor visits and prescriptions to vision and dental care, plus long-term services like nursing home care or home modifications if your parent can't live safely at home.

Income limits are strict — generally around $1,215 per month for a single person, following federal SSI standards. If your parent receives SSI, they automatically qualify without additional income testing. Assets must stay under $2,000 for an individual or $3,000 for a couple, though their home is typically protected.

For long-term care services like nursing home coverage or home waivers, your parent must also meet Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements — a clinical assessment showing they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management.

exemptAssets: Primary home (if spouse/disabled child lives there)
Burial funds up to certain limits

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care services, must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care — a clinical assessment showing need for daily assistance with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other basic activities
otherRequirements: Must be Montana resident
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Aged (65+), blind, or disabled per Social Security Administration criteria

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at apply.mt.gov or contact your local DPHHS office — processing time varies based on disability determination needs.

steps.title: Check if your parent already receives SSI
steps.description: If your parent gets Supplemental Security Income (SSI), they automatically qualify for ABD Medicaid. Contact your local DPHHS office to enroll.

steps.title: Apply online or contact DPHHS
steps.description: Submit application at apply.mt.gov or contact your local DPHHS Human and Community Services Division office. Find offices at dphhs.mt.gov.

steps.title: Submit required documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of age/disability, income, assets, residency, and citizenship. Medical documentation may require Social Security Administration review.

steps.title: Complete disability determination if needed
steps.description: If not already receiving SSI or Social Security Disability, your parent may need medical evaluation to confirm disability status.

steps.title: Assessment for long-term care services
steps.description: If applying for nursing home or waiver services, complete Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment to determine functional needs.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent receives SSI, bring their award letter — this automatically qualifies them for ABD Medicaid without additional income testing.

documentsNeeded: Social Security card for applicant
Birth certificate, passport, or other proof of age
Social Security award letter if receiving SSI or SSDI
Proof of Montana residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
U.S. citizenship documentation or immigration papers
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Pay stubs or other income statements (last 30 days)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Vehicle titles and registration for all vehicles
Property deeds or tax statements
Life insurance policies with cash values
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Medical records documenting disability (if not receiving SSI/SSDI)
Medicare card if applicable
Current health insurance information

contacts.label: Montana 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all social services and benefit programs

contacts.label: DPHHS Office Locator
contacts.description: Find your local Human and Community Services Division office at dphhs.mt.gov

applicationNotes: SSI recipients automatically qualify — bring your award letter to speed up enrollment
If income exceeds limits, ask about medically needy spenddown which allows qualification by deducting medical expenses
Home equity up to $752,000 is protected if your spouse, disabled child, or child under 21 lives there
Working disabled individuals may qualify for MWD (Medicaid for Working Disabled) with higher income limits up to 250% of federal poverty level

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than the $2,000 asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, the home is exempt if your parent, their spouse, a disabled child, or a child under 21 lives there. However, home equity cannot exceed $752,000 (2026 limit). If your parent moves to a nursing home permanently, the home may need to be sold unless an exempt family member continues living there.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: Your parent may still qualify through the medically needy program by 'spending down' excess income on medical expenses. Montana calculates how much income exceeds the limit, then your parent can deduct medical costs to meet the threshold. Contact your local DPHHS office to discuss spenddown options.

faqs.question: Does receiving ABD Medicaid affect my parent's Social Security or Medicare benefits?
faqs.answer: No, ABD Medicaid works alongside Social Security and Medicare without reducing those benefits. In fact, if your parent has Medicare, ABD Medicaid can help pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and co-pays. This coordination often results in better overall coverage.

faqs.question: Can I apply for ABD Medicaid while my parent is in the hospital?
faqs.answer: Yes, and you should apply as soon as possible. Hospital discharge planners can often help initiate the application process. ABD Medicaid can cover hospital stays retroactively up to three months before the application date, potentially saving thousands in medical bills.

faqs.question: What's the difference between regular ABD and MWD (Medicaid for Working Disabled)?
faqs.answer: MWD allows higher income limits (up to $3,261/month for individuals in 2025) but requires ongoing work activity each month and may include cost-sharing fees. Regular ABD has stricter income limits but no work requirements. MWD also allows higher asset limits and excludes retirement accounts from asset calculations.

faqs.question: How long does the disability determination process take if my parent isn't on SSI?
faqs.answer: Disability determinations by the Social Security Administration typically take 3-6 months, though this can be longer if additional medical evidence is needed. If your parent has a terminal illness or meets certain severe conditions, they may qualify for expedited processing within days or weeks.

faqs.question: Can my parent get long-term care at home instead of a nursing facility?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent meets Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements, they may qualify for home and community-based waiver services. These can include home modifications, personal care assistance, and other services to help them stay at home safely. Waiver services may have waiting lists depending on funding availability.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's condition improves and they no longer meet disability requirements?
faqs.answer: Montana conducts periodic reviews to confirm continued eligibility. If your parent no longer meets disability criteria but is 65 or older, they can continue receiving ABD Medicaid under the aged category. If under 65 and no longer disabled, they would need to qualify under different Medicaid categories or lose coverage.

programs.name: Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), Qualifying Individual (QI)
programs.tagline: If your parent's income is under $1,761/month, Montana will pay their Medicare premiums and potentially all Medicare out-of-pocket costs.

localEntities.name: Local DPHS/OPA offices in each Montana county/tribe; find via dphhs.mt.gov.

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to afford Medicare premiums, deductibles, or copays, Montana's Medicare Savings Programs can eliminate these costs entirely. Because these programs pay Medicare expenses directly to providers, your parent gets full Medicare coverage without the financial stress — QMB covers all Medicare out-of-pocket costs, while SLMB and QI cover at least the monthly Part B premium ($185 in 2026).

The programs have three tiers based on income: QMB for those earning up to $1,305/month, SLMB for $1,305–$1,565/month, and QI for $1,565–$1,761/month. Because all three tiers automatically qualify your parent for Extra Help with prescription drug costs, they'll also pay minimal amounts for medications. Asset limits apply ($9,950 for individuals, $14,910 for couples), but the primary home and one car don't count toward this limit.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums ($2,220/year) plus deductibles and coinsurance for QMB tier

summary: Must have Medicare Part A (or be eligible)

exemptAssets: Life insurance with face value under $1,500

otherRequirements: Must have Medicare Part A or be eligible for it
Montana residency required
Must be U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your local Montana DPHS office by phone, online, or in person — processing takes up to 45 days.

steps.title: Contact Montana Medicaid
steps.description: Call 1-800-362-1504 or visit dphhs.mt.gov to find your local county DPHS office. You can also apply online through the Montana Access Portal.

steps.title: Submit application and documents
steps.description: Complete the Medicare Savings Program application with all required income, asset, and identity documents. Submit by mail, online, or in person at your local office.

steps.title: Wait for processing
steps.description: QMB applications take up to 45 days and coverage starts the first of the month after approval. SLMB and QI can be retroactive up to 3 months.

steps.title: Receive coverage confirmation
steps.description: Once approved, Montana will pay Medicare costs directly to providers. You'll also automatically receive Extra Help for prescription drug costs.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Up to 45 days for QMB (no retroactive coverage), SLMB and QI effective first of application month with up to 3 months retroactive coverage
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI may have federal funding limits and close enrollment if funds are exhausted (first-come, first-served)
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early in the year for QI since federal funding can run out — and even if income seems slightly over the limit, the $20 general income disregard and SNAP benefit exclusion may still qualify your parent.

documentsNeeded: Medicare card showing Part A eligibility or enrollment
Social Security award letter or most recent statement
Pay stubs from last 30 days (if still working)
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Retirement account statements (401k, IRA, pensions)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities)
Property deeds or tax statements for any real estate
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with face values
Birth certificate or other proof of age
Proof of Montana residency (utility bill, lease, state ID)
Citizenship documentation (birth certificate, passport, naturalization papers)
SNAP benefit letter (if receiving food stamps)
Pre-need burial contracts or burial trusts

contacts.label: Montana Medicaid Helpline
contacts.description: General Medicaid and Medicare Savings Program questions

contacts.label: Local DPHS Office
contacts.description: Find your county office for in-person applications and assistance

applicationNotes: QMB has no retroactive coverage — apply before the month you need coverage to start
SLMB and QI can provide up to 3 months of retroactive coverage if you were eligible during that time
QI funding is limited federally and may close enrollment if exhausted — apply early in the year
Providers cannot legally bill QMB recipients for Medicare cost-sharing, but some may try — know your rights
Annual Cost of Living Adjustments (COLA) are disregarded for QMB to prevent loss of eligibility

content.benefits: Pays Medicare Part A premiums (if any), Part B premiums, all deductibles, and all coinsurance. Providers cannot bill you for any Medicare-covered services.
Pays Medicare Part B premiums only ($185/month in 2026). You're still responsible for deductibles and coinsurance.
Pays Medicare Part B premiums only ($185/month in 2026). You're still responsible for deductibles and coinsurance.

contentSections.content: All three programs automatically qualify you for Medicare Part D Extra Help, which covers most prescription drug costs. This benefit alone can save $3,000+ annually for people taking multiple medications.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home where your parent lives doesn't count toward the $9,950 asset limit, regardless of its value. Only additional real estate, bank accounts, investments, and retirement funds count toward the limit.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: Apply anyway. Montana automatically deducts $20 from monthly income when calculating eligibility, and SNAP benefits don't count as income at all. Cost of Living Adjustments are also disregarded for QMB recipients to prevent loss of coverage.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my parent if they have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of elderly or disabled parents. You'll need to provide all the same documentation, and Montana may ask for proof that you're authorized to act on their behalf, such as power of attorney.

faqs.question: What happens if a doctor's office tries to bill my QMB-enrolled parent?
faqs.answer: This is illegal. QMB recipients cannot be billed for Medicare deductibles, coinsurance, or copays by any provider. Contact the Montana Medicaid office at 1-800-362-1504 if this happens — they will intervene with the provider.

faqs.question: How long does QI funding typically last each year?
faqs.answer: QI has federal funding caps and operates first-come, first-served. While Montana hasn't specified when funding typically runs out, applying in January or February gives you the best chance of approval before funds are exhausted.

faqs.question: Can my parent have all three programs at the same time?
faqs.answer: No, these are three different income tiers of the same program. Your parent will qualify for whichever tier matches their income level — QMB provides the most comprehensive coverage for those with the lowest incomes.

faqs.question: What happens to coverage if my parent's income changes during the year?
faqs.answer: You must report income changes promptly to Montana Medicaid. If income drops, your parent might qualify for a higher tier (like moving from SLMB to QMB). If income increases above limits, coverage may end, but you'll receive advance notice.

faqs.question: Does Montana take money from the estate after my parent dies?
faqs.answer: No. Since 2010, federal law prohibits estate recovery for Medicare Savings Program benefits. Montana cannot claim repayment from your parent's estate for premiums or cost-sharing paid by these programs.

programs.name: SNAP (Food Stamps)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery assistance for seniors on fixed incomes — up to hundreds per month on an EBT card.

localEntities.name: Local DPHHS Human and Community Services Division offices across Montana

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling with grocery costs, SNAP provides monthly benefits loaded onto an EBT card that works like a debit card at most grocery stores. Montana has expanded eligibility for seniors — households with someone 60+ can qualify with gross monthly income up to $2,608 for one person or $3,526 for two people (2026 limits).

Benefit amounts vary based on net income after deductions, but many seniors qualify for $100-300+ monthly. Because Montana allows higher income limits for elderly households and doesn't count retirement accounts or your home as assets, many seniors who think they won't qualify actually do. Medical expenses over $35/month can be deducted from income, which often makes the difference for qualification.

The program covers most grocery items including fresh produce, meat, dairy, and pantry staples. You cannot use SNAP for alcohol, tobacco, hot prepared foods, or non-food items like soap or vitamins.
programs.savingsSource: Estimated based on typical benefit amounts for senior households

summary: Assets below $4,500 if over 200% poverty level
Montana resident with valid immigration status
otherRequirements: Montana resident
U.S. citizen or qualifying immigration status
Social Security number for all applying household members
Must include spouses and children under 22 in same household

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at apply.mt.gov or call 1-855-642-8650 — processing typically takes 30 days, or 7 days for expedited cases.

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Apply online at apply.mt.gov (fastest), call the DPHHS helpline at 1-855-642-8650, or visit your local DPHHS office. Find office locations at dphhs.mt.gov/HCSD/SNAP.

steps.title: Complete the SNAP application
steps.description: Fill out the full application with income, expense, and household information. Include all people who live together and buy/prepare food together.

steps.title: Submit required documents
steps.description: Provide proof of income, expenses, ID, and Social Security numbers. Medical expenses over $35/month for seniors can be deducted, so include those receipts.

steps.title: Complete eligibility interview
steps.description: DPHHS will schedule a phone interview to verify your information and answer questions about your application.

steps.title: Receive decision and EBT card
steps.description: If approved, you'll receive an EBT card in the mail within 7-10 days. Benefits are loaded monthly and can be used at most grocery stores and farmers markets.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Typically 30 days; expedited within 7 days if very low income or no resources
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has medical bills over $35/month, gather those receipts — they can significantly lower countable income and increase benefit amount.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID (driver's license, state ID, or passport)
Social Security cards for all household members applying
Proof of Montana residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or mail from government agency)
Social Security award letters or benefit statements
Pay stubs from the last 30 days (if working)
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Rent receipts or mortgage statements
Utility bills (electric, gas, water, phone)
Medical bills and receipts over $35/month (for seniors 60+)
Health insurance premium statements
Child support or spousal support documentation (if applicable)
Self-employment records (if applicable)
Vehicle registration and titles (if assets are close to limit)

contacts.label: DPHHS Benefits Helpline
contacts.description: Main number for SNAP applications and questions

contacts.label: DPHHS General Line
contacts.description: General Department of Health and Human Services assistance

contacts.label: Montana 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free information and referral service for all social services

applicationNotes: Expedited processing available within 7 days if household has less than $150 monthly income and $100 or less in cash/bank accounts
Medical expenses over $35/month for seniors can be deducted from income — this often makes the difference between qualifying and not qualifying
Asset limits only apply if gross income exceeds 200% of poverty level — many seniors won't face asset tests at all
Households with all members 60+ or disabled don't have work requirements and get special deductions

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and car and still qualify for SNAP?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's home and vehicles are exempt from SNAP asset limits. Only cash, bank accounts, and non-exempt property count toward the $4,500 asset limit, and that limit only applies if income exceeds 200% of poverty level ($2,608/month for one person).

faqs.question: What if my parent's Social Security puts them over the income limit?
faqs.answer: Montana uses net income after deductions for seniors. Medical expenses over $35/month, shelter costs over half of income, and a standard deduction are subtracted first. Many seniors who appear over the gross limit qualify after these deductions are applied.

faqs.question: Can I apply for SNAP on behalf of my elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent authorizes you as their representative. You'll need to complete an authorized representative form during the application process. Your parent will still need to participate in the eligibility interview unless they're unable to due to disability.

faqs.question: How much will my parent actually receive in SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Benefit amounts depend on net income after deductions. Roughly every $100 in net monthly income reduces benefits by about $30. The exact amount varies by household size and expenses, but many seniors receive $100-300+ monthly.

faqs.question: Can my parent use SNAP benefits at farmers markets?
faqs.answer: Yes, most farmers markets in Montana accept EBT cards. Many also offer matching programs where SNAP dollars are doubled for fresh produce purchases. Check with individual markets about their EBT policies.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income changes after they're approved?
faqs.answer: Report significant changes within 10 days to your local DPHHS office. Increases might reduce benefits, but decreases could increase them. Annual recertification is required, typically 12 months after approval for elderly households.

faqs.question: Can my parent get SNAP if they live in senior housing that provides meals?
faqs.answer: It depends on the meal arrangement. If your parent receives prepared meals as part of their housing, they may not qualify. But if they have their own kitchen and buy/prepare their own food, they can still qualify for SNAP.

faqs.question: What if the application is taking longer than 30 days?
faqs.answer: Contact your local DPHHS office immediately. If the delay is due to missing paperwork, submit documents quickly. If it's a processing delay, ask about interim benefits while waiting for final determination.

programs.name: Montana Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Help your parent get one-time payments to cover heating bills and emergency energy costs during Montana winters.

localEntities.name: Action for Eastern Montana (serves eastern Montana)
Montana Department of Public Health and Human Services (DPHHS) - statewide administration
Local eligibility offices throughout Montana

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay heating bills during Montana winters, LIHEAP provides direct payments to utility companies to help cover energy costs. This federal program assists low-income households with one-time heating payments and emergency energy assistance when furnaces break or utility shutoffs threaten.

Your parent may qualify with monthly income up to $33,719 for a single person or $44,095 for a two-person household in 2025-2026. The program prioritizes households with seniors over 60, and families already receiving SNAP benefits are automatically eligible. Applications are only accepted October 1 through April 30 each year — Montana doesn't provide cooling assistance, only heating help.

otherRequirements: Must pay for heating costs (renters or homeowners eligible)
All household members must be included on application
Cannot have dependents living in other states
Households receiving SNAP are automatically eligible

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at apply.mt.gov or call 1-800-551-3191 if your parent is over 60 or disabled — processing time varies but apply early as funding is limited.

steps.title: Choose your application method
steps.description: Apply online at apply.mt.gov, call 1-800-551-3191 if your parent is 60+ or disabled, or visit your local eligibility office.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect recent fuel bills, income records for all household members 18+, Social Security numbers, and housing information.

steps.title: Submit during application window
steps.description: Applications are only accepted October 1 through April 30. Apply as early as possible since funding is limited and some offices stop accepting applications when funds run out.

steps.title: Wait for benefit payment
steps.description: If approved, LIHEAP pays your parent's utility company directly. Crisis assistance for emergencies may be processed faster than regular heating assistance.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — varies by local office and funding availability
applicationGuide.waitlist: Funding is limited; some local agencies may stop accepting applications earlier if funds run out
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent receives SNAP, mention this on the application — SNAP households are categorically eligible and get priority processing.

documentsNeeded: Recent copy of fuel bill (unless heating with wood or coal)
Income records for all household members age 18+ for the month prior to application
Social Security number for all household members
Housing and utility payment information (utility bills, utility contract, lease agreement)
Financial aid information
Landlord's contact information (if renting)

contacts.label: LIHEAP Senior Hotline
contacts.description: Application assistance for households with members over 60 or with disabilities

contacts.label: Montana DPHHS
contacts.description: Statewide program administration

contacts.label: Action for Eastern Montana
contacts.description: LIHEAP administration for eastern Montana regions

applicationNotes: Crisis LIHEAP is available year-round for energy emergencies like broken furnaces or utility shutoff notices — this is separate from regular heating assistance
All household members age 18+ must sign the application, and roommates sharing utility bills count as the same household even if they don't share other expenses

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they're a renter who doesn't pay utilities directly?
faqs.answer: Your parent must pay heating costs to qualify, whether as a renter or homeowner. If utilities are included in rent, they may not be eligible unless they can show they're responsible for heating costs. Check with your local LIHEAP office about specific rental situations.

faqs.question: What counts as a crisis that qualifies for emergency assistance?
faqs.answer: Crisis LIHEAP covers energy emergencies like a broken furnace, utility shutoff notice, or running out of heating fuel. This emergency assistance is available year-round, unlike regular LIHEAP which only accepts applications October through April.

faqs.question: If my parent lives with roommates, how does that affect eligibility?
faqs.answer: All people sharing the same utility bill count as one LIHEAP household, even if they don't share other expenses. This means roommates' income and assets will be counted together for eligibility, which could help or hurt depending on everyone's financial situation.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Free home energy improvements that can cut your parent's heating bills by hundreds of dollars per year.

localEntities.name: Action for Eastern Montana (17 counties), Human Resource Council, CAPNM, Action Inc. Butte

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles with high heating bills or lives in a drafty home, Montana's Weatherization Assistance Program provides free energy efficiency improvements including insulation, air sealing, and heating system repairs. The program serves homeowners and renters whose income is at or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level ($30,120 for a single person in 2026).

Because this program is tied to LIHEAP (Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program), your parent must first qualify for energy assistance — households receiving SNAP, SSI, or TANF are automatically eligible. Priority goes to homes with elderly residents, people with disabilities, or young children, based on energy burden and home condition.

The program uses a priority waitlist system managed by regional providers across Montana. If approved but not immediately served, families must reapply after one year. Applications are accepted October 1 through April 30 for the standard cycle, though local offices may accept applications year-round.

summary: Income at or below 200% Federal Poverty Level
otherRequirements: Must be LIHEAP eligible (at or below 60% state median income) or qualify for Weatherization Only (up to 200% FPL)
Home must not have been previously weatherized
Renters need signed landlord access agreement (form DPHHS-EAP-013)
U.S. citizen, qualified alien, or lawful permanent resident with Social Security number verification
Households on SNAP, SSI, or TANF are automatically income-eligible

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your regional provider using the combined LIHEAP/Weatherization application, with most applications accepted October 1 through April 30.

steps.title: Find your regional provider
steps.description: Montana uses regional sub-grantees. For eastern Montana (17 counties including Carter, Custer, Dawson, Fallon): Action for Eastern Montana at 2030 N. Merrill Ave, Glendive MT 59330. For western areas: Human Resource Council at 406-728-3710. For Butte area: Action Inc. serving Beaverhead, Deer Lodge, Granite, Madison, Powell, and Silver Bow counties.

steps.title: Get the combined application
steps.description: Request the 2025-2026 combined LIHEAP and Weatherization application from your local provider. This single form covers both programs since Weatherization requires LIHEAP eligibility first.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of income from the last 30 days, recent fuel/heating bills, citizenship documentation, and Social Security numbers for all household members. If renting, get form DPHHS-EAP-013 (landlord access agreement) signed by your parent's landlord.

steps.title: Submit during application period
steps.description: Applications are primarily accepted October 1 through April 30, but local offices may accept them year-round. Submit by mail, phone, fax (e.g., 406-377-3571 for some areas), or drop-off at your regional office.

steps.title: Wait for priority ranking
steps.description: If approved, you'll be placed on a priority list based on income level, energy burden, and vulnerable household members (elderly, disabled, children). Check your position with your local provider.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — priority-based system means timeline varies by ranking and available funding
applicationGuide.waitlist: Priority-based waitlist ranked by need — elderly and disabled households get special priority. If not served within one year, must reapply.
applicationGuide.tip: Households already receiving SNAP, SSI, or TANF automatically meet income requirements but still need to submit the application and required documentation.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID for applicant
Social Security cards for all household members
Proof of citizenship, qualified alien status, or lawful permanent residency
Proof of income from last 30 days (pay stubs, Social Security award letter, pension statements)
Recent fuel or heating bill
Recent electric bill or other utility bills
Bank statements if applicable for resource verification
SNAP, SSI, or TANF award letters if receiving these benefits
Signed landlord access agreement (form DPHHS-EAP-013) if renting
Documentation of all household members and their dates of birth

contacts.label: Human Resource Council
contacts.description: Weatherization services for western Montana counties

contacts.label: Action for Eastern Montana
contacts.description: Serves 17 eastern counties including Carter, Custer, Dawson, Fallon, Garfield, McCone, Phillips
contacts.hours: Contact via mail: 2030 N. Merrill Ave, Glendive MT 59330

contacts.label: Montana 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free referral service to find your local weatherization provider

applicationNotes: The application period runs primarily October 1 through April 30, but some local offices accept applications year-round — call your regional provider to confirm
Renters must get their landlord to sign the access agreement (form DPHHS-EAP-013) before work can begin — this is often the biggest hurdle for rental properties
If you're approved but placed on the waitlist, you must reapply after one year if no services were provided
Households receiving SNAP, SSI, or TANF are automatically income-eligible but still need to complete the full application process
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
Low Income Household Water Assistance Program

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they rent their home?
faqs.answer: Yes, but the landlord must sign form DPHHS-EAP-013 (Montana Weatherization Assistance Program Access Agreement) giving permission for the work. This signed agreement must be submitted with the application. Many rental applications are delayed or denied because landlords won't sign this form.

faqs.question: What if my parent's home was weatherized years ago?
faqs.answer: Homes that have been previously weatherized through any federal weatherization program are not eligible. There is no time limit — if weatherization work was done 10 or 20 years ago, the home still cannot receive services again.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: There's no guaranteed timeline because the program uses priority ranking rather than first-come-first-served. Elderly and disabled households get priority, along with those with the highest energy burden. If your parent isn't served within one year of approval, you must reapply and go through the ranking process again.

faqs.question: Can I apply for both LIHEAP and Weatherization at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, Montana uses a combined application for both programs. In fact, you must be LIHEAP-eligible to qualify for Weatherization, so most applicants need both programs. The same application covers energy bill assistance and home efficiency improvements.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income is slightly above the LIHEAP limit?
faqs.answer: There's a 'Weatherization Only' option for households up to 200% of the Federal Poverty Level ($30,120 for a single person in 2026), even if they don't qualify for LIHEAP energy bill assistance. However, LIHEAP-eligible households get priority on the waitlist.

faqs.question: Can a family member apply on behalf of an elderly parent?
faqs.answer: Yes, but the application must include the elderly parent's information and documentation since eligibility is based on their household income and circumstances. The parent may need to sign forms or provide consent depending on your local provider's requirements.

faqs.question: What exactly does 'energy efficiency improvements' include?
faqs.answer: Services typically include insulation installation, air sealing, heating system repairs or replacements, weatherstripping, storm windows, and other upgrades identified during a home energy audit. The specific work depends on your parent's home condition and energy needs as determined by the weatherization crew.

faqs.question: What if we miss the October 1 - April 30 application period?
faqs.answer: Contact your local provider directly — some offices accept applications year-round even though the official cycle runs October through April. Regional offices like Human Resource Council (406-728-3710) can tell you their specific policies for out-of-cycle applications.

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.tagline: Free Medicare counseling to help your parent choose the right plans and avoid costly mistakes

localEntities.name: Administered by Montana Office on Aging via Area Agencies on Aging statewide; local examples include Roosevelt County SHIP Counselors and Richland County Council on Aging

programs.intro:
If your parent has Medicare or is approaching 65, they need objective guidance to navigate plan choices, enrollment deadlines, and coverage gaps. Montana's State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) provides free, one-on-one counseling to help your parent understand their options without sales pressure from insurance companies.

SHIP counselors help with Medicare enrollment, compare Part D prescription plans, explain Medigap options, and connect families to programs like Extra Help that can reduce medication costs. Because counselors aren't affiliated with insurance companies, they provide unbiased advice focused on your parent's specific health needs and budget. Home visits are available for homebound individuals.

summary: Medicare beneficiary, family member, or caregiver
otherRequirements: Must be seeking help with Medicare-related issues or low-income programs

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide number and you'll be connected to a local counselor for immediate help.

steps.title: Call the Montana SHIP hotline
steps.description: Call 1-800-551-3191 and you'll be connected to your local Area Agency on Aging counselor. No appointment needed for basic questions.

steps.title: Schedule detailed counseling if needed
steps.description: For complex situations or plan comparisons, your counselor will schedule a longer session by phone, in-person, or home visit.

steps.title: Bring relevant documents to your session
steps.description: Have your parent's Medicare card, current insurance details, prescription list, and income information if applying for low-income programs.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate phone response with connection to local counselor; appointment scheduling varies by local availability
applicationGuide.tip: Call during open enrollment (October 15 - December 7) as early as possible — counselors get busiest near the deadline.

contacts.label: Montana SHIP Statewide
contacts.description: Connects you to your local Area Agency on Aging SHIP counselor
contacts.hours: Business hours, varies by local office

contacts.label: Montana Office on Aging
contacts.description: State office for general SHIP program questions

faqs.question: Can I get help for my parent if they live in a nursing home?
faqs.answer: Yes, SHIP counselors can meet with nursing home residents and their families. Call 1-800-551-3191 to arrange counseling at the facility or discuss your parent's Medicare options over the phone.

faqs.question: Will the counselor try to sell us insurance?
faqs.answer: No. SHIP counselors are not affiliated with insurance companies and do not sell any products. Their job is to provide objective information to help you make the best decision for your parent's situation and budget.

faqs.question: Can we get help outside of open enrollment periods?
faqs.answer: Yes, SHIP counselors are available year-round for Medicare questions, fraud concerns, and help with qualifying life events that trigger special enrollment periods. You don't have to wait for open enrollment to get help.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels
programs.tagline: Hot, nutritious meals delivered to your parent's home 5 days a week if they're 60+ and unable to cook for themselves.

localEntities.name: Cascade County: 406-454-6993, Great Falls
Western MT Area VI Agency: programs@area6aging.org, local senior centers
Mom's Meals for Medicaid waivers statewide via case managers

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggles to prepare meals due to health issues, mobility problems, or recovery from hospitalization, Meals on Wheels delivers hot, nutritious meals directly to their door. Each meal meets half of daily nutritional requirements and follows low-salt, low-fat guidelines.

Meals are delivered Monday through Friday between 10am and 12:30pm by volunteer drivers who provide an informal safety check during each visit. The suggested donation is around $4.50 per meal, but your parent can pay what they're able — income is not a barrier to receiving services. Because delivery is limited to specific service areas, you'll need to confirm your parent lives within a local provider's delivery zone.
programs.savingsSource: Based on suggested donation of $4.50/meal for 5 meals/week over 52 weeks

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound or unable to cook/prepare meals due to disability, mobility issues, health conditions, or post-hospital recovery
otherRequirements: Must live within local provider's delivery zone
Home visit assessment required to determine meal preparation ability and dietary needs

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local provider for a home visit assessment — service can start within a week if no waitlist.

steps.title: Contact your local provider
steps.description: Call your area's Meals on Wheels provider. In Cascade County/Great Falls, call 406-454-6993 or 406-454-6990. For western Montana, contact Area VI Agency on Aging at programs@area6aging.org.

steps.title: Schedule home assessment
steps.description: A staff member will visit your parent's home to assess their age, health, mobility, meal preparation ability, and dietary restrictions. They'll also collect emergency contact information.

steps.title: Confirm delivery details
steps.description: If approved, arrange delivery schedule and discuss suggested donation amount based on your parent's financial situation.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Service can begin within a week in most areas, longer if there's a waitlist
applicationGuide.waitlist: Some programs have waitlists that may extend processing time
applicationGuide.tip: Your parent's doctor, social worker, or hospital discharge planner can also make referrals to speed up the process.

documentsNeeded: Proof of age (birth certificate, driver's license, or state ID)
Proof of residence within delivery zone (utility bill or lease agreement)
List of current medications and dietary restrictions
Emergency contact information for family or friends
Medical information if disability or health condition affects meal preparation
Insurance information if applicable (Medicare, Medicaid)

contacts.label: Cascade County (Great Falls area)
contacts.description: Meals on Wheels service for Cascade County residents

contacts.label: Area VI Agency on Aging
contacts.description: Western Montana Meals on Wheels programs
contacts.hours: Contact local senior centers for hours

contacts.label: Montana Aging Services
contacts.description: Free referral service to find local providers statewide

applicationNotes: Service areas are limited — confirm your parent lives within delivery boundaries before applying
If your parent can leave home or has others who can cook for them, they may not qualify for home delivery
Medicaid waiver participants may qualify for free meals through Mom's Meals program — contact their case manager
Service typically requires 5-day-per-week commitment, though short-term post-hospital delivery is available

faqs.question: What if my parent lives outside a delivery zone?
faqs.answer: Meals on Wheels is not available statewide in Montana. If your parent lives outside a service area, contact 2-1-1 for alternatives like senior center congregate meals, grocery delivery services, or the Commodity Supplemental Food Program. Some areas may have volunteer driver programs for grocery shopping.

faqs.question: Can my parent get meals if they can still drive but have trouble cooking?
faqs.answer: Probably not. Home-delivered meals are specifically for homebound seniors or those unable to prepare meals due to disability or health conditions. If your parent can drive, they should be referred to congregate meals at local senior centers instead.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent can't afford the suggested donation?
faqs.answer: Your parent will still receive meals. The $4.50 suggested donation is adjusted based on ability to pay, and many participants pay less or nothing at all. Income is not a factor for eligibility in Montana programs. Medicaid waiver participants may qualify for completely free meals through separate programs.

faqs.question: How long can my parent receive Meals on Wheels?
faqs.answer: There's no time limit as long as your parent remains homebound and unable to prepare meals. However, if their condition improves, the program encourages transitioning to congregate meals at senior centers to promote socialization. Regular reassessments determine continued need.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs meals on weekends or holidays?
faqs.answer: Most Montana programs only deliver Monday through Friday. Weekends and holidays typically have no delivery. Some programs may provide shelf-stable emergency meals for these gaps, but coverage varies by provider. Ask your local program about weekend meal options when you apply.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my parent if they have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can initiate the application process. The home assessment will evaluate your parent's cognitive ability to live safely at home and manage meal delivery. If they have significant dementia, the assessor will work with you to determine if home delivery is appropriate or if other care arrangements are needed.

programs.name: Respite Care (via Big Sky Waiver)
programs.tagline: Up to 30 days per year of temporary care for your parent so you can take a break from caregiving.

localEntities.name: Mountain Pacific (statewide coordinator)
County OPA offices

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, Montana's Big Sky Waiver provides respite care services that give you temporary relief from caregiving. You can get up to 30 days per year of professional care in your parent's home, your home, or a community setting — with extensions possible for health and safety needs.

This isn't a standalone program. Your parent must first qualify for Montana Medicaid and meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment proving they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management). Because the waiver covers comprehensive home and community-based services, your parent gets a full care team rather than just respite hours.

Respite care is delivered as part of a person-centered care plan that may also include personal assistance, homemaker services, adult day health, nursing, and therapies. The program operates statewide through county offices, but waiver slots are limited and waitlists are common.

summary: Nursing home level of care required
Physical disabilities or at risk of institutionalization
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment proving your parent needs the same level of daily assistance provided in a nursing home — typically help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Montana Medicaid
Must have unmet care needs that can only be addressed through waiver services
Must need at least one waiver service per month
Must be elderly with physical disabilities or at risk of nursing home placement

applicationGuide.summary: Call Mountain Pacific at (800) 219-7035 for waiver referral and your county OPA office for Medicaid enrollment — processing time not specified but expect waitlists.

steps.title: Apply for Montana Medicaid first
steps.description: Contact your county Office of Public Assistance to start Medicaid enrollment. You must be approved for Medicaid before applying for waiver services.

steps.title: Call Mountain Pacific for waiver referral
steps.description: Call (800) 219-7035 or (406) 443-4020 to request Big Sky Waiver services. They coordinate the program statewide.

steps.title: Complete Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment
steps.description: A clinical team will assess your parent's functional needs through an in-person or phone evaluation to determine if they meet nursing home level of care requirements.

steps.title: Wait for waiver slot availability
steps.description: Due to program caps, you may be placed on a waitlist. Priority is typically given based on immediate safety needs and risk of institutionalization.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time not specified in program materials
applicationGuide.waitlist: Waitlists are likely due to waiver slot caps, though specific wait times are not published
applicationGuide.tip: Start the Medicaid application process early — you cannot access waiver services without Medicaid approval first.

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for all household members
Medicare card (both parts A and B)
Birth certificate or other proof of age
Proof of Montana residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Medical records documenting functional limitations and care needs
Current medication list and prescribing physician information
Documentation of any other income sources (pensions, retirement accounts)
Property documents if you own real estate besides primary home
Vehicle titles and registration for all vehicles
Life insurance policies with cash values
Documentation of current care arrangements and unmet needs
Power of attorney or guardianship papers if applying on behalf of parent

contacts.label: Mountain Pacific (Waiver Services)
contacts.description: Statewide coordinator for Big Sky Waiver referrals and services

contacts.label: Mountain Pacific Helena Office
contacts.description: Local Mountain Pacific office for waiver coordination

contacts.label: Montana 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all social services and county office locations

applicationNotes: You must be approved for Medicaid before you can even apply for waiver services — start that process immediately
The clinical assessment is thorough and looks at your parent's ability to safely live at home without institutional care
Waiver slots are limited statewide, so even if your parent qualifies, they may face a waitlist
Extensions beyond 30 days of respite per year are possible for health and safety reasons, but require case-by-case approval
relatedPrograms: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
Senior and Long Term Care Division services

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if it's worth more than $2,000?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is completely exempt from asset limits regardless of value. Only other assets like bank accounts, investments, and additional property count toward the $2,000 individual limit.

faqs.question: What if my parent has Medicaid but doesn't meet nursing home level of care?
faqs.answer: They cannot access Big Sky Waiver services. The nursing facility level of care requirement is strict — your parent must need the same daily assistance provided in a nursing home. Consider other Medicaid home services or Area Agency on Aging programs instead.

faqs.question: Can I get respite care immediately in a crisis situation?
faqs.answer: No, there's no emergency respite provision. Your parent must complete the full application process, clinical assessment, and potentially wait for an available waiver slot. For immediate crisis support, contact your county OPA about other emergency services.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Montana doesn't publish specific waitlist times, but waiver programs typically have months to years-long waits depending on your region and your parent's priority level. Those at immediate risk of nursing home placement often get priority.

faqs.question: Can I choose where the respite care happens?
faqs.answer: Yes, respite can be provided in your parent's home, your family's home, the care provider's home, or community settings. The location is part of your person-centered care planning process.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's needs change after enrollment?
faqs.answer: The care team will reassess and adjust services as needed. If your parent's condition improves significantly and they no longer meet nursing facility level of care, they may lose waiver eligibility.

faqs.question: Can we apply for both Big Sky Waiver and PACE at the same time?
faqs.answer: No, you cannot be enrolled in both programs simultaneously. PACE is an alternative to the waiver program. You'll need to choose which program better fits your parent's needs and service preferences.

faqs.question: Does the 30-day respite limit reset every year?
faqs.answer: Yes, you get up to 30 days annually. However, the program documents don't specify if this follows a calendar year or enrollment anniversary year — ask Mountain Pacific when you apply for clarification.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 55+ and struggling to find work, they can earn minimum wage while gaining job skills through community service positions.

localEntities.name: Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain (primary sub-grantee for MT)
Montana DLI Workforce Services Division (oversight)
Local Job Service Montana offices

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55 or older and unemployed, SCSEP provides paid work experience at community organizations like schools, hospitals, and senior centers. Your parent earns minimum wage for an average of 20 hours per week while receiving job training and placement assistance.

Because SCSEP combines immediate income with skills training, participants work toward unsubsidized employment in the private sector. The program lasts 6-12 months and includes resume workshops, interview training, and computer skills development. To qualify, your parent must be unemployed or underemployed with income at or below 125% of the federal poverty level.

In Montana, the program is administered by Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain, with limited federally-funded slots that often create waitlists. Veterans, people over 65, and those with disabilities receive priority placement.

summary: Income at or below 125% federal poverty level
otherRequirements: Must be unemployed or underemployed
U.S. work authorization required
Priority given to veterans and qualified spouses, individuals over 65, those with disabilities, low literacy skills, limited English proficiency, rural residents, homeless or at risk of homelessness

applicationGuide.summary: Contact Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain directly or call the national SCSEP helpline — processing time varies due to limited slots.

steps.title: Contact the Montana SCSEP provider
steps.description: Call Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain or visit their website at www.esgw.org/scsep to inquire about current openings and begin the application process.

steps.title: Call the national helpline for assistance
steps.description: If you need help finding local programs or have questions, call the national SCSEP toll-free line at 1-877-872-5627 (1-877-US2-JOBS).

steps.title: Visit your local Job Service Montana office
steps.description: Get referrals and orientation information at any Job Service Montana office statewide, or contact the Montana Department of Labor & Industry Workforce Services Division.

steps.title: Complete eligibility documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of age, income verification showing you're at or below 125% of federal poverty level, and proof of U.S. work authorization. Priority documentation (veteran status, disability) is optional but recommended.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Likely due to limited federally-allocated slots; varies by region and provider capacity
applicationGuide.tip: Because slots are limited, apply as soon as possible and ask about priority status if you're a veteran, over 65, or have a disability.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID showing age 55 or older
Proof of U.S. work authorization (Social Security card, passport, or employment authorization document)
Income verification for last 30 days (pay stubs, Social Security award letter, unemployment benefits statement)
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Proof of any other income (pension statements, retirement benefits, disability payments)
Veteran discharge papers (DD-214) if claiming veteran priority
Disability documentation if claiming disability priority
Proof of current address (utility bill, lease agreement, or mail)

contacts.label: National SCSEP Helpline
contacts.description: National toll-free helpline for SCSEP information and local program referrals

contacts.label: Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain
contacts.description: Montana's SCSEP sub-grantee - check www.esgw.org/scsep for contact information

contacts.label: Montana Department of Labor & Industry
